summary: For Most Investors, Mutual Funds ही काफी हैं. In this episode of This or That Podcast, Neil Borate, one of India’s most respected personal-finance journalists and authors, joins Bhartendra Singh to decode what truly matters in investing.

seo.title: Why Mutual Funds Are Enough For Most Investors?
seo.description: For Most Investors, Mutual Funds ही काफी हैं. In this episode of This or That Podcast, Neil Borate, one of India’s most respected personal-finance

takeaways.text: Neil Borate makes the case that for most investors mutual funds are enough, and around 90% of people do not need PMS, AIF or other complex products.
Return expectations should stay grounded: the episode argues that 20% returns are a fantasy, not something to build a plan around.
A core-satellite portfolio is offered as a simple way to keep your investments balanced without piling on complexity.
Global investing and GIFT City are treated as increasingly relevant for Indian investors who want exposure beyond home markets.
NPS still works even under the new tax regime, and the real edge comes from behaviour rather than the product you pick.

fork.decision: Should I move beyond mutual funds into PMS, AIF and complex products, or are mutual funds enough?

points.text: For roughly 90% of investors, mutual funds cover what they actually need, without PMS, AIF or complex products.
A simple core-satellite portfolio built with mutual funds stays balanced and easy to manage.
Behaviour matters more than product, and mutual funds let you focus on staying the course instead of chasing complexity.
PMS and AIF are marketed as more sophisticated options for investors who feel plain mutual funds are not enough.
The pull is often FOMO and the hope of outsized returns like 20%, which the episode calls a fantasy.

fork.pick: For about 90% of investors, mutual funds are enough. Skip PMS and AIF, build a simple core-satellite portfolio, keep your return expectations realistic, and spend your energy on behaviour rather than chasing complex products.
